From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Pedro IV of Portugal](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_IV_of_Portugal&redirect=no "Pedro IV of Portugal"))

Emperor of Brazil (1822–31) and King of Portugal (1826)

This article is about the emperor of Brazil who also reigned as Pedro IV of Portugal. For his ancestor, see [Peter I of Portugal](/wiki/Peter_I_of_Portugal "Peter I of Portugal").

"Dom Pedro I" redirects here. For the steam ship of the same name, see [*Wyreema*](/wiki/Wyreema "Wyreema").

*[Dom](/wiki/Don_(honorific) "Don (honorific)")* **Pedro I**[[a]](#cite_note-3) (12 October 1798 – 24 September 1834), known in Brazil and in Portugal as "**the Liberator**" ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *o Libertador*) or "**the Soldier King**" (*o Rei Soldado*) in Portugal,[[b]](#cite_note-6) was the founder and [first ruler](/wiki/List_of_monarchs_of_Brazil "List of monarchs of Brazil") of the [Empire of Brazil](/wiki/Empire_of_Brazil "Empire of Brazil") from 1822 to 1831 (under the name of Pedro I) and [King of Portugal](/wiki/List_of_Portuguese_monarchs#House_of_Braganza_(1640–1910) "List of Portuguese monarchs") in 1826 (under the name of **Pedro IV**).

Born in [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon"), Pedro was the fourth child of King Dom [John VI of Portugal](/wiki/John_VI_of_Portugal "John VI of Portugal") and Queen [Carlota Joaquina](/wiki/Carlota_Joaquina "Carlota Joaquina"), and thus a member of the [House of Braganza](/wiki/House_of_Braganza "House of Braganza"). When the country was [invaded](/wiki/Invasion_of_Portugal_(1807) "Invasion of Portugal (1807)") by French troops in 1807, he and his family fled to Portugal's largest and wealthiest colony, Brazil. The outbreak of the [Liberal Revolution of 1820](/wiki/Liberal_Revolution_of_1820 "Liberal Revolution of 1820") in Lisbon compelled Pedro I's father to return to Portugal in April 1821, leaving him to rule Brazil as regent. He had to deal with challenges from revolutionaries and insubordination by Portuguese troops, all of which he subdued. The Portuguese government's threat to revoke the political autonomy that Brazil had enjoyed since 1808 was met with widespread discontent in Brazil. Pedro I chose the Brazilian side and declared [Brazil's independence](/wiki/Independence_of_Brazil "Independence of Brazil") from Portugal on 7 September 1822. On 12 October, he was acclaimed Emperor and by March 1824 had [defeated all armies loyal to Portugal](/wiki/Brazilian_War_of_Independence "Brazilian War of Independence"). A few months later, Pedro I crushed the short-lived [Confederation of the Equator](/wiki/Confederation_of_the_Equator "Confederation of the Equator"), a failed secession attempt by provincial rebels in Brazil's [northeast](/wiki/Northeast_Region,_Brazil "Northeast Region, Brazil").

A secessionist rebellion in the southern province of [Cisplatina](/wiki/Cisplatina "Cisplatina") in early 1825, and the subsequent attempt by the [United Provinces of the Río de la Plata](/wiki/United_Provinces_of_the_R%C3%ADo_de_la_Plata "United Provinces of the Río de la Plata") to annex it, led the Empire into the [Cisplatine War](/wiki/Cisplatine_War "Cisplatine War"). In March 1826, Pedro I briefly became king of Portugal before abdicating in favor of his eldest daughter, Dona [Maria II](/wiki/Maria_II_of_Portugal "Maria II of Portugal"). The situation worsened in 1828 when the war in the south resulted in Brazil's loss of Cisplatina. During the same year in Lisbon, Maria II's throne was usurped by Prince Dom [Miguel](/wiki/Miguel_I_of_Portugal "Miguel I of Portugal"), Pedro I's younger brother. The Emperor's concurrent and scandalous sexual affair with [Domitila de Castro](/wiki/Domitila_de_Castro,_Marchioness_of_Santos "Domitila de Castro, Marchioness of Santos") tarnished his reputation. Other difficulties arose in the [Brazilian parliament](/wiki/General_Assembly_(Brazil) "General Assembly (Brazil)"), where a struggle over whether the government would be chosen by the monarch or by the legislature dominated political debates from 1826 to 1831. Unable to deal with problems in both Brazil and Portugal simultaneously, on 7 April 1831 Pedro I [abdicated](/wiki/Abdication_of_Pedro_I_of_Brazil "Abdication of Pedro I of Brazil") in favor of his son [Dom Pedro II](/wiki/Pedro_II_of_Brazil "Pedro II of Brazil"), and sailed for Europe.

Pedro I invaded Portugal at the head of an army in July 1832. Faced at first with what seemed a national civil war, he soon became involved in a wider conflict that enveloped the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") in a struggle between proponents of liberalism and those seeking a return to [absolutism](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy"). Pedro I died of [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis") in September 1834, just a few months after he and the liberals had emerged victorious. He was hailed by both contemporaries and posterity as a key figure who helped spread the liberal ideals that allowed Brazil and Portugal to move from absolutist regimes to representative forms of government.

## Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_I_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early years")]

### Birth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_I_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Birth")]

[![Painting with a half-length portrait of a young child with wavy auburn hair, wearing a blue jacket, open-necked lace-trimmed shirt, and striped sash, and holding a small bouquet of flowers](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Dom_Pedro_I_1800.jpg/250px-Dom_Pedro_I_1800.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dom_Pedro_I_1800.jpg)

Pedro around age 2, c.1800, by [Agustín Esteve](/wiki/Agust%C3%ADn_Esteve "Agustín Esteve")

Pedro was born at 08:00 on 12 October 1798 in the [Queluz Royal Palace](/wiki/Queluz_Royal_Palace "Queluz Royal Palace") near [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon"), Portugal.[[5]](#cite_note-7) He was named after St. [Peter of Alcantara](/wiki/Peter_of_Alcantara "Peter of Alcantara"), and his full name was Pedro de Alcântara Francisco António João Carlos Xavier de Paula Miguel Rafael Joaquim José Gonzaga Pascoal Cipriano Serafim.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBranco1838XXXVI-1)[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon19753-2) He was referred to using the honorific "[Dom](/wiki/Dom_(title) "Dom (title)")" ([Lord](/wiki/Lord "Lord")) from birth.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999424-8)

Through his father, Prince Dom John (later King Dom [John VI](/wiki/John_VI_of_Portugal "John VI of Portugal")), Pedro was a member of the [House of Braganza](/wiki/House_of_Braganza "House of Braganza") ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Bragança*) and a grandson of King Dom [Peter III](/wiki/Peter_III_of_Portugal "Peter III of Portugal") and Queen Dona (Lady) [Maria I of Portugal](/wiki/Maria_I_of_Portugal "Maria I of Portugal"), who were uncle and niece as well as husband and wife.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon19505,_9,_11-9)[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_15,_9–10-10) His mother, Doña [Carlota Joaquina](/wiki/Carlota_Joaquina "Carlota Joaquina"), was the daughter of King [Don](/wiki/Don_(honorific) "Don (honorific)") [Charles IV](/wiki/Charles_IV_of_Spain "Charles IV of Spain") of Spain.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon195012-11) Pedro's parents had an unhappy marriage. Carlota Joaquina was an ambitious woman, who always sought to advance Spain's interests, even to the detriment of Portugal's. Reputedly unfaithful to her husband, she went as far as to plot his overthrow in league with dissatisfied Portuguese nobles.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_14,_8,_10,_28-12)[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon195012–13-13)

As the second eldest son (though the fourth child), Pedro became his father's heir apparent and [Prince of Beira](/wiki/Prince_of_Beira "Prince of Beira") upon the death of his elder brother [Francisco António](/wiki/Francisco_Ant%C3%B3nio,_Prince_of_Beira "Francisco António, Prince of Beira") in 1801.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay19866-14) Prince Dom John had been acting as regent on behalf of his mother, Queen Maria I, after she was declared incurably insane in 1792.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay19863-15)[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_19-16) By 1802, Pedro's parents were estranged; John lived in the [Mafra Palace](/wiki/Mafra_National_Palace "Mafra National Palace") and Carlota Joaquina in [Ramalhão Palace](/wiki/Ramalh%C3%A3o_Palace "Ramalhão Palace").[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay19867-17)[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_112-18) Pedro and his siblings resided in the Queluz Palace with their grandmother Maria I, far from their parents, whom they saw only during state occasions at Queluz.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay19867-17)[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_112-18)

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_I_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Education")]

[![Painting showing the head and shoulders of a boy wearing a high collar and a coat adorned with medals and a striped sash of office](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Dom_Pedro_I_1809c.png/250px-Dom_Pedro_I_1809c.png)](/wiki/File:Dom_Pedro_I_1809c.png)

Pedro around age 11, c.1809, by [Francesco Bartolozzi](/wiki/Francesco_Bartolozzi "Francesco Bartolozzi")

In late November 1807, when Pedro was nine, [the royal family escaped from Portugal](/wiki/Transfer_of_the_Portuguese_Court_to_Brazil "Transfer of the Portuguese Court to Brazil") as an invading French army sent by [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") approached Lisbon. Pedro and his family arrived in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), then capital of [Brazil](/wiki/State_of_Brazil "State of Brazil"), Portugal's largest and wealthiest colony, in March 1808.[[17]](#cite_note-19) During the voyage, Pedro read [Virgil](/wiki/Virgil "Virgil")'s *[Aeneid](/wiki/Aeneid "Aeneid")* and conversed with the ship's crew, picking up [navigational](/wiki/Navigation "Navigation") skills.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_139,_41-20)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay198622-21) In Brazil, after a brief stay in the [City Palace](/wiki/Pa%C3%A7o_Imperial "Paço Imperial"), Pedro settled with his younger brother [Miguel](/wiki/Miguel_I_of_Portugal "Miguel I of Portugal") and their father in the [Palace of São Cristóvão](/wiki/Palace_of_S%C3%A3o_Crist%C3%B3v%C3%A3o "Palace of São Cristóvão") (Saint Christopher).[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay198629-22) Although never on intimate terms with his father, Pedro loved him and resented the constant humiliation his father suffered at the hands of Carlota Joaquina due to her extramarital affairs.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay19867-17)[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_1125,_128-23) As an adult, Pedro would openly call his mother, for whom he held only feelings of contempt, a "bitch".[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986189-24) The early experiences of betrayal, coldness and neglect had a great impact on the formation of Pedro's character.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay19867-17)

A modicum of stability during his childhood was provided by his *aia* (governess), Maria Genoveva do Rêgo e Matos, whom he loved as a mother, and by his *aio* (supervisor) friar António de Arrábida, who became his mentor.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon195033-25)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay198622,_33-26) Both were in charge of Pedro's upbringing and attempted to furnish him with a suitable education. His instruction encompassed a broad array of subjects that included mathematics, [political economy](/wiki/Political_economy "Political economy"), logic, history and geography.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay198632-27) He learned to speak and write not only in [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), but also [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") and [French](/wiki/French_language "French language").[[26]](#cite_note-28) He could translate from [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and understood [German](/wiki/German_language "German language").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECosta1995101-29) Even later on, as an emperor, Pedro would devote at least two hours of each day to study and reading.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECosta1995101-29)[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_1121-30)

Despite the breadth of Pedro's instruction, his education proved lacking. Historian [Octávio Tarquínio de Sousa](/wiki/Oct%C3%A1vio_Tarqu%C3%ADnio_de_Sousa "Octávio Tarquínio de Sousa") said that Pedro "was without a shadow of doubt intelligent, quick-witted, [and] perspicacious."[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_2101-31) However, historian Roderick J. Barman relates that he was by nature "too ebullient, too erratic, and too emotional". He remained impulsive and never learned to exercise self-control or to assess the consequences of his decisions and adapt his outlook to changes in situations.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman199917-32) His father never allowed anyone to discipline him.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay198632-27) While Pedro's schedule dictated two hours of study each day, he sometimes circumvented the routine by dismissing his instructors in favor of activities that he found more interesting.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay198632-27)

### First marriage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_I_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: First marriage")]

[![Colored full-length portrait showing a young man with curly hair and long sideburns who is wearing an elaborate gold-embroidered blue military tunic with gold epaulets and medals, blue trousers, black boots, a striped sash of office and gold belt, with his left hand resting on a sheathed sword](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Emperor_Pedro_I_1816.jpg/250px-Emperor_Pedro_I_1816.jpg)](/wiki/File:Emperor_Pedro_I_1816.jpg)

Portrait by [Jean-Baptiste Debret](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Debret "Jean-Baptiste Debret") of Pedro around age 18, c.1816

The prince found fulfillment in activities that required physical skills, rather than in the classroom. At his father's [Santa Cruz farm](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_Estate "Santa Cruz Estate"), Pedro [trained unbroken horses](/wiki/Horse_training "Horse training"), and became a fine horseman and an excellent [farrier](/wiki/Farrier "Farrier").[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay198646-33)[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELustosa200658-34) He and his brother Miguel enjoyed mounted hunts over unfamiliar ground, through forests, and even at night or in inclement weather.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay198646-33) He displayed a talent for drawing and handicrafts, applying himself to wood carving and furniture making.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay198636-35) In addition, he had a taste for music, and under the guidance of [Marcos Portugal](/wiki/Marcos_Portugal "Marcos Portugal") the prince became an able composer (later creating Brazil's [Independence Anthem](/wiki/Hino_da_Independ%C3%AAncia "Hino da Independência")). He had a good singing voice, and was proficient with several musical instruments (including [piano](/wiki/Piano "Piano"), [flute](/wiki/Flute "Flute") and [guitar](/wiki/Guitar "Guitar")), playing popular songs and dances.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay198637-36) Pedro was a simple man, both in habits and in dealing with others. Except on solemn occasions when he donned court dress, his daily attire consisted of white cotton trousers, striped cotton jacket and a broad-brimmed straw hat, or a frock coat and a top hat in more formal situations.[[35]](#cite_note-37) He would frequently take time to engage in conversation with people on the street, noting their concerns.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986177-38)

Pedro's character was marked by an energetic drive that bordered on hyperactivity. He was impetuous with a tendency to be domineering and short-tempered. Easily bored or distracted, he entertained himself with dalliances with women in addition to his hunting and equestrian activities.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988134-39) His restless spirit compelled him to search for adventure, and, sometimes in disguise as a traveler, he frequented taverns in Rio de Janeiro's disreputable districts.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_1252-40)[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay198651-41) He rarely drank alcohol, but was an incorrigible womanizer.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELustosa200671-42)[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_176-43) His earliest known lasting affair was with a French dancer called Noémi Thierry, who had a stillborn child by him. Pedro's father, who had ascended the throne as John VI, sent Thierry away to avoid jeopardizing the prince's betrothal to Archduchess [Maria Leopoldina](/wiki/Maria_Leopoldina "Maria Leopoldina"), daughter of Emperor Francis I of Austria (formerly [Francis II, Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Francis_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Francis II, Holy Roman Emperor")).[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_178–80-44)[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay198653-45)

On 13 May 1817, Pedro was [married by proxy](/wiki/Married_by_proxy "Married by proxy") to Maria Leopoldina.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECosta197242-46)[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon195044-47) When she arrived in Rio de Janeiro on 5 November, she immediately fell in love with Pedro, who was far more charming and attractive than she had been led to expect. After "years under a tropical sun, his complexion was still light, his cheeks rosy." The 19-year-old prince was handsome and a little above average in height, with bright dark eyes and dark brown hair.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay198646-33) "His good appearance", said historian [Neill Macaulay](/wiki/Neill_W._Macaulay,_Jr. "Neill W. Macaulay, Jr."), "owed much to his bearing, proud and erect even at an awkward age, and his grooming, which was impeccable. Habitually neat and clean, he had taken to the Brazilian custom of bathing often."[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay198646-33) The Nuptial Mass, with the ratification of the vows previously taken by proxy, occurred the following day.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_196-48) Seven children resulted from this marriage: Maria (later Queen Dona [Maria II of Portugal](/wiki/Maria_II_of_Portugal "Maria II of Portugal")), [Miguel](/wiki/Miguel,_Prince_of_Beira "Miguel, Prince of Beira"), [João](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_Carlos,_Prince_of_Beira "João Carlos, Prince of Beira"), [Januária](/wiki/Princess_Janu%C3%A1ria_of_Brazil "Princess Januária of Brazil"), [Paula](/wiki/Princess_Paula_of_Brazil "Princess Paula of Brazil"), [Francisca](/wiki/Princess_Francisca_of_Brazil "Princess Francisca of Brazil") and Pedro (later Emperor Dom [Pedro II of Brazil](/wiki/Pedro_II_of_Brazil "Pedro II of Brazil")).[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon195049-49)

## Independence of Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_I_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Independence of Brazil")]

Main article: [Independence of Brazil](/wiki/Independence_of_Brazil "Independence of Brazil")

### Liberal Revolution of 1820

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_I_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Liberal Revolution of 1820")]

Main article: [Liberal Revolution of 1820](/wiki/Liberal_Revolution_of_1820 "Liberal Revolution of 1820")

[![Colored sketch depicting a crowd of civilian and military figures standing and waving before the crowded balcony of a pedimented building with people looking on from its windows](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Oath_of_obedience_by_Prince_Pedro_1821.jpg/500px-Oath_of_obedience_by_Prince_Pedro_1821.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oath_of_obedience_by_Prince_Pedro_1821.jpg)

Pedro, in his father's name, makes an oath of obedience to the Portuguese Constitution on 26 February 1821. He can be seen at the middle of the balcony raising his hat. Painting by [Félix Taunay, Baron of Taunay](/wiki/F%C3%A9lix_Taunay,_Baron_of_Taunay "Félix Taunay, Baron of Taunay").

On 17 October 1820, news arrived that the military garrisons in Portugal had mutinied, leading to what became known as the [Liberal Revolution of 1820](/wiki/Liberal_Revolution_of_1820 "Liberal Revolution of 1820"). The military formed a provisional government, supplanting the regency appointed by John VI, and summoned the *Cortes*—the centuries-old Portuguese parliament, this time democratically elected with the aim of creating a national Constitution.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman198864-50) Pedro was surprised when his father not only asked for his advice, but also decided to send him to Portugal to rule as regent on his behalf and to placate the revolutionaries.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman198868-51) The prince was never educated to rule and had previously been allowed no participation in state affairs. The role that was his by birthright was instead filled by his elder sister Dona [Maria Teresa](/wiki/Teresa,_Princess_of_Beira "Teresa, Princess of Beira"): John VI had relied on her for advice, and it was she who had been given membership in the [Council of State](/wiki/Privy_council "Privy council").[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay198647–48-52)

Pedro was regarded with suspicion by his father and by the king's close advisers, all of whom clung to the principles of [absolute monarchy](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy"). By contrast, the prince was a well-known, staunch supporter of [liberalism](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism") and of constitutional representative monarchy. He had read the works of [Voltaire](/wiki/Voltaire "Voltaire"), [Benjamin Constant](/wiki/Benjamin_Constant "Benjamin Constant"), [Gaetano Filangieri](/wiki/Gaetano_Filangieri "Gaetano Filangieri") and [Edmund Burke](/wiki/Edmund_Burke "Edmund Burke").[[51]](#cite_note-53) Even his wife Maria Leopoldina remarked, "My husband, God help us, loves the new ideas."[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_1123-54)[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay198693-55) John VI postponed Pedro's departure for as long as possible, fearing that once he was in Portugal, he would be acclaimed king by the revolutionaries.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman198868-51)

On 26 February 1821, Portuguese troops stationed in Rio de Janeiro mutinied. Neither John VI nor his government made any move against the mutinous units. Pedro decided to act on his own and rode to meet the rebels. He negotiated with them and convinced his father to accept their demands, which included naming a new cabinet and making an oath of obedience to the forthcoming [Portuguese Constitution](/wiki/Portuguese_Constitution_of_1822 "Portuguese Constitution of 1822").[[54]](#cite_note-56) On 21 April, the parish electors of Rio de Janeiro met at the Merchants' Exchange to elect their representatives to the *Cortes*. A small group of agitators seized the meeting and formed a revolutionary government. Again, John VI and his ministers remained passive, and the monarch was about to accept the revolutionaries' demands when Pedro took the initiative and sent army troops to re-establish order.[[55]](#cite_note-57) Under pressure from the *Cortes*, John VI and his family departed for Portugal on 26 April, leaving behind Pedro and Maria Leopoldina.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman198872-58) Two days before he embarked, the King warned his son: "Pedro, if Brazil breaks away, let it rather do so for you, who will respect me, than for one of those adventurers."[[57]](#cite_note-59)

### Independence or Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_I_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Independence or Death")]

[![Painted head and shoulders portrait showing a young man with curly hair and mustachios who is wearing a formal black coat, high collar and cravat with a city scene in the distant background](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Emperor_Dom_Pedro_I_1822.png/250px-Emperor_Dom_Pedro_I_1822.png)](/wiki/File:Emperor_Dom_Pedro_I_1822.png)

Pedro at age 23 with the town of [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo") in the background, August 1822 by [Simplício Rodrigues de Sá](/wiki/Simpl%C3%ADcio_Rodrigues_de_S%C3%A1 "Simplício Rodrigues de Sá")

At the outset of his regency, Pedro promulgated decrees that guaranteed personal and property rights. He also reduced government expenditure and taxes.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay198693-55)[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_1232–233-60) Even the revolutionaries arrested in the Merchants' Exchange incident were set free.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay198696-61) On 5 June 1821, army troops under Portuguese lieutenant general [Jorge Avilez](/wiki/Jorge_Avilez,_1st_Count_of_Avilez "Jorge Avilez, 1st Count of Avilez") (later Count of Avilez) mutinied, demanding that Pedro should take an oath to uphold the Portuguese Constitution after it was enacted. The prince rode out alone to intervene with the mutineers. He calmly and resourcefully negotiated, winning the respect of the troops and succeeding in reducing the impact of their more unacceptable demands.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman198874-62)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELustosa2006114-63) The mutiny was a thinly veiled military coup d'état that sought to turn Pedro into a mere figurehead and transfer power to Avilez.[[62]](#cite_note-64) The prince accepted the unsatisfactory outcome, but he also warned that it was the last time he would yield under pressure.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELustosa2006114-63)[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_1242-65)

The continuing crisis reached a point of no return when the *Cortes* dissolved the central government in Rio de Janeiro and ordered Pedro's return.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_1264-66)[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman198881-67) This was perceived by Brazilians as an attempt to subordinate their country again to Portugal—Brazil had not been a colony since 1815 and had the [status of a kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Portugal,_Brazil_and_the_Algarves "United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves").[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_1264–265-68)[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman198882-69) On 9 January 1822, Pedro was presented with a petition containing 8,000 signatures that begged him not to leave.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman198883-70)[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986107-71) He replied, "Since it is for the good of all and the general happiness of the Nation, I am willing. Tell the people that I am staying."[[70]](#cite_note-72) The date became known as the *[Dia do Fico](/wiki/Dia_do_Fico "Dia do Fico")*.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELustosa200698-73) Avilez again mutinied and tried to force Pedro's return to Portugal. This time the prince fought back, rallying the Brazilian troops (which had not joined the Portuguese in previous mutinies),[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman198878-74) militia units and armed civilians.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman198884-75)[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986109–110-76) Outnumbered, Avilez surrendered and was expelled from Brazil along with his troops.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986116-77)[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon195085-78)

During the next few months, Pedro attempted to maintain a semblance of unity with Portugal, but the final rupture was impending. Aided by an able minister, [José Bonifácio de Andrada](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Bonif%C3%A1cio_de_Andrada "José Bonifácio de Andrada"), he searched for support outside Rio de Janeiro. The prince traveled to [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais") in April and on to [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)") in August. He was welcomed warmly in both Brazilian provinces, and the visits reinforced his authority.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman198890–91,_96-79)[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986119,_122–123-80) While returning from São Paulo, he received news sent on 7 September that the *Cortes* would not accept [self-governance](/wiki/Self-governance "Self-governance") in Brazil and would punish all who disobeyed its orders.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986124-81) "Never one to eschew the most dramatic action on the immediate impulse", said Barman about the prince, he "required no more time for decision than the reading of the letters demanded."[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman198896-82) Pedro mounted his bay mare[[c]](#cite_note-93) and, in front of his entourage and his Guard of Honor, said:

> Friends, the Portuguese *Cortes* wished to enslave and persecute us. As of today our bonds are ended. By my blood, by my honor, by my God, I swear to bring about the independence of Brazil. Brazilians, let our watchword from this day forth be 'Independence or Death!'[[91]](#cite_note-94)

### Constitutional Emperor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_I_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Constitutional Emperor")]

[![Half-length pencil or silverpoint sketch showing a young man with curly hair and long sideburns facing left who is wearing an elaborate embroidered military tunic with heavy gold epaulets, sash and medals](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Pedro_I_Imperador_1823_%28crop%29.png/250px-Pedro_I_Imperador_1823_%28crop%29.png)](/wiki/File:Pedro_I_Imperador_1823_(crop).png)

Profile view of Pedro I at age 25 by [Debret](/wiki/Debret "Debret"), 1823

The prince was acclaimed Emperor Dom Pedro I on his 24th birthday, which coincided with the inauguration of the [Empire of Brazil](/wiki/Empire_of_Brazil "Empire of Brazil") on 12 October. He was [crowned](/wiki/Coronations_in_the_Americas#Brazil "Coronations in the Americas") on 1 December in what is today known as the [Old Cathedral of Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Old_Cathedral_of_Rio_de_Janeiro "Old Cathedral of Rio de Janeiro"). His ascendancy did not immediately extend throughout Brazil's territories. He had to force the submission of several provinces in the [northern](/wiki/North_Region,_Brazil "North Region, Brazil"), [northeastern](/wiki/Northeast_Region,_Brazil "Northeast Region, Brazil") and [southern](/wiki/South_Region,_Brazil "South Region, Brazil") regions, and the last Portuguese holdout units only surrendered in early 1824.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1994420–422-95)[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988104–106-96) Meanwhile, Pedro I's relationship with Bonifácio deteriorated.[[94]](#cite_note-97) The situation came to a head when Pedro I, on the grounds of inappropriate conduct, dismissed Bonifácio. Bonifácio had used his position to harass, prosecute, arrest and even exile his political enemies.[[95]](#cite_note-98) For months Bonifácio's enemies had worked to win over the Emperor. While Pedro I was still Prince Regent, they had given him the title "Perpetual Defender of Brazil" on 13 May 1822.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman198892-99) They also inducted him into Freemasonry on 2 August and later made him grand master on 7 October, replacing Bonifácio in that position.[[97]](#cite_note-100)

The crisis between the monarch and his former minister was felt immediately within the [Constituent and Legislative General Assembly](/wiki/Brazilian_Constituent_Assembly_(1823) "Brazilian Constituent Assembly (1823)"), which had been elected for the purpose of drafting a Constitution.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986120-101) A member of the Constituent Assembly, Bonifácio resorted to demagoguery, alleging the existence of a major Portuguese conspiracy against Brazilian interests—insinuating that Pedro I, who had been born in Portugal, was implicated.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986153–154-102)[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988116-103) The Emperor became outraged by the invective directed at the loyalty of citizens who were of Portuguese birth and the hints that he was himself conflicted in his allegiance to Brazil.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988117-104) On 12 November 1823, Pedro I ordered the [dissolution of the Constituent Assembly](/wiki/Night_of_Agony "Night of Agony") and called for new elections.[[102]](#cite_note-105) On the following day, he placed a newly established native [Council of State](/wiki/Privy_council "Privy council") in charge of composing a constitutional draft. Copies of the draft were sent to all town councils, and the vast majority voted in favor of its instant adoption as the [Constitution of the Empire](/wiki/Brazilian_Constitution_of_1824 "Brazilian Constitution of 1824").[[103]](#cite_note-106)

As a result of the highly centralized State created by the Constitution, rebellious elements in [Ceará](/wiki/Cear%C3%A1 "Ceará"), [Paraíba](/wiki/Para%C3%ADba "Paraíba") and [Pernambuco](/wiki/Pernambuco "Pernambuco") attempted to secede from Brazil and unite in what became known as the [Confederation of the Equator](/wiki/Confederation_of_the_Equator "Confederation of the Equator").[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986165-107)[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988122-108) Pedro I unsuccessfully sought to avoid bloodshed by offering to placate the rebels.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986165-107)[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988121-109) Angry, he said: "What did the insults from Pernambuco require? Surely a punishment, and such a punishment that it will serve as an example for the future."[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986165-107) The rebels were never able to secure control over their provinces, and were easily suppressed. By late 1824, the rebellion was over.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988122-108)[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986166-110) Sixteen rebels were tried and executed,[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986166-110)[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988278-111) while all others were pardoned by the Emperor.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1994435-112)

## Crises within and without

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_I_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Crises within and without")]

### Portuguese dynastic affair

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_I_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Portuguese dynastic affair")]

[![Painted half-length portrait showing a young man with curly hair and mustachios who is wearing an elaborate embroidered military tunic with gold epaulets and medals](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Ant%C3%B4nio_Joaquim_Franco_Velasco_-_Dom_Pedro_I%2C_Imperador_do_Brasil.jpg/250px-Ant%C3%B4nio_Joaquim_Franco_Velasco_-_Dom_Pedro_I%2C_Imperador_do_Brasil.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ant%C3%B4nio_Joaquim_Franco_Velasco_-_Dom_Pedro_I,_Imperador_do_Brasil.jpg)

Pedro I at age 27 during his trip to [Salvador, Bahia](/wiki/Salvador,_Bahia "Salvador, Bahia") province, March 1826, by [Antônio Joaquim Franco Velasco](/w/index.php?title=Ant%C3%B4nio_Joaquim_Franco_Velasco&action=edit&redlink=1 "Antônio Joaquim Franco Velasco (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ant%C3%B4nio_Joaquim_Franco_Velasco "pt:Antônio Joaquim Franco Velasco")]

After long negotiations, Portugal [signed a treaty](/wiki/Treaty_of_Rio_de_Janeiro_(1825) "Treaty of Rio de Janeiro (1825)") with Brazil on 29 August 1825 in which it recognized Brazilian independence.[[110]](#cite_note-113) Except for the recognition of independence, the treaty provisions were at Brazil's expense, including a demand for reparations to be paid to Portugal, with no other requirements of Portugal. Compensation was to be paid to all Portuguese citizens residing in Brazil for the losses they had experienced, such as properties which had been confiscated. John VI was also given the right to style himself emperor of Brazil.[[111]](#cite_note-114) More humiliating was that the treaty implied that independence had been granted as a beneficent act of John VI, rather than having been compelled by the Brazilians through force of arms.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988140-115)[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_2195-116) Even worse, Great Britain was rewarded for its role in advancing the negotiations by the signing of a separate treaty in which its favorable commercial rights were renewed and by the signing of a convention in which Brazil agreed to abolish slave trade with Africa within four years. Both accords were severely harmful to Brazilian economic interests.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988141-117)[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986186-118)

A few months later, the Emperor received word that his father had died on 10 March 1826, and that he had succeeded his father on the Portuguese throne as King Dom Pedro IV.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988142-119) Aware that a reunion of Brazil and Portugal would be unacceptable to the people of both nations, he hastily abdicated the crown of Portugal on 2 May in favor of his eldest daughter, who became Queen Dona Maria II.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorato183526-120)[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBranco1838XXXVII-121)[[d]](#cite_note-125) His abdication was conditional: Portugal was required to accept the Constitution which he had drafted and Maria II was to marry his brother Miguel.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988142-119) Regardless of the abdication, Pedro I continued to act as an absentee king of Portugal and interceded in its diplomatic matters, as well as in internal affairs, such as making appointments.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988148-126) (Although Maria II had already been recognized as queen, all Portuguese coins for the period 1826–1828 bear the name and image of Pedro IV.) He found it difficult, at the very least, to keep his position as Brazilian emperor separate from his obligations to protect his daughter's interests in Portugal.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988148-126) On 3 March 1828, when his plans appeared to be secure, he finalized his earlier conditional abdication from the Portuguese throne with an additional decree.[[123]](#cite_note-127)

Miguel feigned compliance with Pedro I's plans. As soon as he was declared regent in early 1828, and backed by Carlota Joaquina, he abrogated the Constitution and, supported by those Portuguese in favor of absolutism, was acclaimed King Dom Miguel I.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986226-128) As painful as was his beloved brother's betrayal, Pedro I also endured the defection of his surviving sisters, [Maria Teresa](/wiki/Teresa,_Princess_of_Beira "Teresa, Princess of Beira"), [Maria Francisca](/wiki/Infanta_Maria_Francisca_of_Portugal "Infanta Maria Francisca of Portugal"), [Isabel Maria](/wiki/Infanta_Isabel_Maria_of_Portugal "Infanta Isabel Maria of Portugal") and [Maria da Assunção](/wiki/Infanta_Maria_da_Assun%C3%A7%C3%A3o_of_Portugal "Infanta Maria da Assunção of Portugal"), to Miguel I's faction.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986295-129) Only his youngest sister, [Ana de Jesus](/wiki/Infanta_Ana_de_Jesus_Maria_of_Portugal "Infanta Ana de Jesus Maria of Portugal"), remained faithful to him, and she later traveled to Rio de Janeiro to be close to him.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986255,_295-130) Consumed by hatred and beginning to believe rumors that Miguel I had murdered their father, Pedro I turned his focus on Portugal and tried in vain to garner international support for Maria II's rights.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986239-131)[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988147–148-132)

### War and widowhood

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_I_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: War and widowhood")]

[![A large crowd of people and mounted horsemen fill a large public square before the steps of a twin-spired baroque church](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Celebration_for_the_return_of_Emperor_Pedro_I_1826.jpg/500px-Celebration_for_the_return_of_Emperor_Pedro_I_1826.jpg)](/wiki/File:Celebration_for_the_return_of_Emperor_Pedro_I_1826.jpg)

Celebration in São Francisco de Paula square, [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro") city, for the return of Pedro I from [Bahia](/wiki/Bahia "Bahia"), 4 April 1826 by [Debret](/wiki/Debret "Debret")

Backed by the [United Provinces of the Río de la Plata](/wiki/United_Provinces_of_the_R%C3%ADo_de_la_Plata "United Provinces of the Río de la Plata") (present-day [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina")), a [small band](/wiki/Thirty-Three_Orientals "Thirty-Three Orientals") declared Brazil's southernmost province of [Cisplatina](/wiki/Cisplatina "Cisplatina") to be independent in April 1825.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988125-133) The Brazilian government at first perceived the secession attempt as a minor uprising. It took months before a greater threat posed by the involvement of the United Provinces, which expected to annex Cisplatina, caused serious concern. In retaliation, the Empire declared war in December, triggering the [Cisplatine War](/wiki/Cisplatine_War "Cisplatine War").[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988128-134) The Emperor traveled to [Bahia](/wiki/Bahia "Bahia") province (located in [northeastern Brazil](/wiki/Northeastern_Brazil "Northeastern Brazil")) in February 1826, taking along his wife and daughter Maria. The Emperor was warmly welcomed by the inhabitants of Bahia.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_2206-135) The trip was planned to generate support for the war-effort.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986190-136)

The imperial entourage included [Domitila de Castro](/wiki/Domitila_de_Castro,_Marchioness_of_Santos "Domitila de Castro, Marchioness of Santos") (then-Viscountess and later Marchioness of Santos), who had been Pedro I's [mistress](/wiki/Royal_mistress "Royal mistress") since their first meeting in 1822. Although he had never been faithful to Maria Leopoldina, he had previously been careful to conceal his sexual escapades with other women.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986168,_190-137) However, his infatuation for his new lover "had become both blatant and limitless", while his wife endured slights and became the object of gossip.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988146-138) Pedro I was increasingly rude and mean toward Maria Leopoldina, left her short of funds, prohibited her from leaving the palace and forced her to endure Domitila's presence as her [lady-in-waiting](/wiki/Lady-in-waiting "Lady-in-waiting").[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELustosa2006192,_231,_236-139)[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman199916-140) In the meantime, his lover took advantage by advancing her interests, as well as those of her family and friends. Those seeking favors or to promote projects increasingly sought her help, bypassing the normal, legal channels.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988136-141)

On 24 November 1826, Pedro I sailed from Rio de Janeiro to [São José](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Jos%C3%A9,_Santa_Catarina "São José, Santa Catarina") in the province of [Santa Catarina](/wiki/Santa_Catarina_(state) "Santa Catarina (state)"). From there he rode to [Porto Alegre](/wiki/Porto_Alegre "Porto Alegre"), capital of the province of [Rio Grande do Sul](/wiki/Rio_Grande_do_Sul "Rio Grande do Sul"), where the main army was stationed.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986201–202-142) Upon his arrival on 7 December, the Emperor found the military conditions to be much worse than previous reports had led him to expect. He "reacted with his customary energy: he passed a flurry of orders, fired reputed grafters and incompetents, fraternized with the troops, and generally shook up military and civilian administration."[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986202-143) He was already on his way back to Rio de Janeiro when he was told that Maria Leopoldina had died following a miscarriage.[[140]](#cite_note-144) Unfounded rumors soon spread that purported that she had died after being physically assaulted by Pedro I.[[e]](#cite_note-152) Meanwhile, the war continued on with no conclusion in sight. Pedro I relinquished Cisplatina in August 1828, and the province became the independent nation of [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay").[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986211-153)[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988151-154)

### Second marriage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_I_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Second marriage")]

[![Under a red canopy in a baroque church, a man in uniform places a ring on the finger of a woman in an elaborate white dress, attended by 4 small children, bishops and other onlookers](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Second_marriage_of_S.M.I._D._Pedro_I.jpg/500px-Second_marriage_of_S.M.I._D._Pedro_I.jpg)](/wiki/File:Second_marriage_of_S.M.I._D._Pedro_I.jpg)

Marriage of Pedro I to [Amélie of Leuchtenberg](/wiki/Am%C3%A9lie_of_Leuchtenberg "Amélie of Leuchtenberg"). Next to him, in [order of precedence](/wiki/Order_of_precedence "Order of precedence"), are his children with Maria Leopoldina: [Pedro](/wiki/Pedro_II_of_Brazil "Pedro II of Brazil"), [Januária](/wiki/Princess_Janu%C3%A1ria_of_Brazil "Princess Januária of Brazil"), [Paula](/wiki/Princess_Paula_of_Brazil "Princess Paula of Brazil") and [Francisca](/wiki/Princess_Francisca_of_Brazil "Princess Francisca of Brazil"). Painting by [Debret](/wiki/Debret "Debret").

After his wife's death, Pedro I realized how miserably he had treated her, and his relationship with Domitila began to crumble. Maria Leopoldina, unlike his mistress, was popular, honest and loved him without expecting anything in return. The Emperor greatly missed her, and even his obsession with Domitila failed to overcome his sense of loss and regret.[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman199924-155) One day Domitila found him weeping on the floor and embracing a portrait of his deceased wife, whose sad-looking ghost Pedro I claimed to have seen.[[151]](#cite_note-156) Later on, the Emperor left the bed he shared with Domitila and shouted: "Get off of me! I know I live an unworthy life of a sovereign. The thought of the Empress does not leave me."[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECosta199588-157)[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERangel1928195-158) He did not forget his children, orphaned of their mother, and was observed on more than one occasion holding his son, the young Pedro, in his arms and saying: "Poor boy, you are the most unhappy prince in the world."[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELustosa2006250-159)

At the insistence of Pedro I, Domitila departed from Rio de Janeiro on 27 June 1828.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELustosa2006262-160) He had resolved to marry again and to become a better person. He even tried to persuade his father-in-law of his sincerity, by claiming in a letter "that all my wickedness is over, that I shall not again fall into those errors into which I have fallen, which I regret and have asked God for forgiveness".[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELustosa2006252-161) Francis I was less than convinced. The Austrian emperor, deeply offended by the conduct his daughter endured, withdrew his support for Brazilian concerns and frustrated Pedro I's Portuguese interests.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988147-151) Because of Pedro I's bad reputation in Europe, owing to his past behavior, princesses from several nations declined his proposals of marriage one after another.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986226-128) His pride thus wounded, he allowed his mistress to return, which she did on 29 April 1829 after having been away nearly a year.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELustosa2006252-161)[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_2320-162)

However, once he learned that a betrothal had finally been arranged, the Emperor ended his relationship to Domitila. She returned to her native province of São Paulo on 27 August, where she remained.[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_2326-163) Days earlier, on 2 August, the Emperor had been married by proxy to [Amélie of Leuchtenberg](/wiki/Am%C3%A9lie_of_Leuchtenberg "Amélie of Leuchtenberg").[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECosta199594-164)[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_38-165) He was stunned by her beauty after meeting her in person.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELustosa2006285-166)[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_315-167) The vows previously made by proxy were ratified in a Nuptial Mass on 17 October.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986235-168)[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERangel1928274-169) Amélie was kind and loving to his children and provided a much needed sense of normality to both his family and the general public.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988156-170) After Domitila's banishment from court, the vow the Emperor made to alter his behavior proved to be sincere. He had no more affairs and remained faithful to his spouse.[[166]](#cite_note-171) In an attempt to mitigate and move beyond other past misdeeds, he made peace with José Bonifácio, his former minister and mentor.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986236-172)[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELustosa2006283-173)

## Between Portugal and Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_I_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Between Portugal and Brazil")]

### Endless crises

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_I_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Endless crises")]

[![Engraved half-length portrait showing a young man with curly hair who is wearing an elaborate brocade military tunic with epaulets, a striped sash of office and medals](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Emperor_Dom_Pedro_I_1830.jpg/250px-Emperor_Dom_Pedro_I_1830.jpg)](/wiki/File:Emperor_Dom_Pedro_I_1830.jpg)

Pedro I at age 32, 1830

Since the days of the Constituent Assembly in 1823, and with renewed vigor in 1826 with the opening of the [General Assembly](/wiki/General_Assembly_(Brazil) "General Assembly (Brazil)") (the Brazilian parliament), there had been an ideological struggle over the balance of powers wielded by the emperor and legislature in governance. On one side were those who shared Pedro I's views, politicians who believed that the monarch should be free to choose ministers, national policies and the direction of government. In opposition were those, then known as the Liberal Party, who believed that cabinets should have the power to set the government's course and should consist of deputies drawn from the majority party who were accountable to the parliament.[[169]](#cite_note-174) Strictly speaking, both the party that supported Pedro I's government and the Liberal Party advocated [Liberalism](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism"), and thus [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy").[[170]](#cite_note-175)

Regardless of Pedro I's failures as a ruler, he respected the Constitution: he did not tamper with elections or countenance vote rigging, refuse to [sign](/wiki/Countersign_(legal) "Countersign (legal)") acts ratified by the government, or impose any restrictions on freedom of speech.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986x,_193,_195,_219,_229,_221-176)[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1994445-177) Although within his prerogative, he did not [dissolve](/wiki/Dissolution_of_parliament "Dissolution of parliament") the Chamber of Deputies and call for new elections when it disagreed with his aims or postpone seating the legislature.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1994476-178) Liberal newspapers and pamphlets seized on Pedro I's Portuguese birth in support of both valid accusations (e.g., that much of his energy was directed toward affairs concerning Portugal)[[174]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986229-179) and false charges (e.g., that he was involved in plots to suppress the Constitution and to reunite Brazil and Portugal).[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986244-180) To the Liberals, the Emperor's Portuguese-born friends who were part of the Imperial court, including [Francisco Gomes da Silva](/wiki/Francisco_Gomes_da_Silva "Francisco Gomes da Silva") who was nicknamed "the Buffoon", were part of these conspiracies and formed a "[secret cabinet](/wiki/Shadow_government_(conspiracy) "Shadow government (conspiracy)")".[[176]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986243-181)[[177]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon1950155–158-182) None of these figures exhibited interest in such issues, and whatever interests they may have shared, there was no palace cabal plotting to abrogate the Constitution or to bring Brazil back under Portugal's control.[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986174-183)

Another source of criticism by the Liberals involved Pedro I's abolitionist views.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986216–217,_246-184) The Emperor had indeed conceived a gradual process for eliminating slavery. However, the constitutional power to enact legislation was in the hands of the Assembly, which was dominated by slave-owning landholders who could thus thwart any attempt at abolition.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986215-185)[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELustosa2006129,_131-186) The Emperor opted to try persuasion by moral example, setting up his estate at Santa Cruz as a model by granting land to his freed slaves there.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986214-187)[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELustosa2006131-188) Pedro I also professed other advanced ideas. When he declared his intention to remain in Brazil on 9 January 1822 and the populace sought to accord him the honor of unhitching the horses and pulling his carriage themselves, the then-Prince Regent refused. His reply was a simultaneous denunciation of the [divine right of kings](/wiki/Divine_right_of_kings "Divine right of kings"), of nobility's supposedly superior blood and of racism: "It grieves me to see my fellow humans giving a man tributes appropriate for the divinity, I know that my blood is the same color as that of the Negroes."[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986108-189)[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELustosa2006128–129-190)

### Abdication

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_I_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Abdication")]

Main article: [Abdication of Pedro I of Brazil](/wiki/Abdication_of_Pedro_I_of_Brazil "Abdication of Pedro I of Brazil")

[![A painting showing a crowded room in which a uniformed man hands a sheaf of papers to another uniformed man while in the background a weeping woman sits in an armchair holding a young boy before whom a woman kneels](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Abdicacao_Pedro_I_do_Brasil.jpg/330px-Abdicacao_Pedro_I_do_Brasil.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abdicacao_Pedro_I_do_Brasil.jpg)

Pedro I delivers his abdication letter on 7 April 1831, painting by [Aurélio de Figueiredo](/w/index.php?title=Aur%C3%A9lio_de_Figueiredo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Aurélio de Figueiredo (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aur%C3%A9lio_de_Figueiredo "pt:Aurélio de Figueiredo")]

The Emperor's efforts to appease the Liberal Party resulted in very important changes. He supported an 1827 law that established [ministerial responsibility](/wiki/Ministerial_responsibility "Ministerial responsibility").[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986195-191) On 19 March 1831, he named a cabinet formed by politicians drawn from the opposition, allowing a greater role for the parliament in the government.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988159-192) Lastly, he offered positions in Europe to Francisco Gomes and another Portuguese-born friend to extinguish rumors of a "secret cabinet".[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988156-170)[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_344-193) To his dismay, his palliative measures did not stop the continuous attacks from the Liberal side upon his government and his foreign birth. Frustrated by their intransigence, he became unwilling to deal with his deteriorating political situation.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988156-170)

Meanwhile, Portuguese exiles campaigned to convince him to give up on Brazil and instead devote his energies to the fight for his daughter's claim to Portugal's crown.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988157-194) According to Roderick J. Barman, "[in] an emergency the Emperor's abilities shone forth—he became cool in nerve, resourceful and steadfast in action. Life as a constitutional monarch, full of tedium, caution, and conciliation, ran against the essence of his character."[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988138-195) On the other hand, the historian remarked, he "found in his daughter's case everything that appealed most to his character. By going to Portugal he could champion the oppressed, display his chivalry and self-denial, uphold constitutional rule, and enjoy the freedom of action he craved."[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988157-194)

The idea of abdicating and returning to Portugal took root in his mind, and, beginning in early 1829, he talked about it frequently.[[191]](#cite_note-196) An opportunity soon appeared to act upon the notion. Radicals within the Liberal Party rallied street gangs to harass the Portuguese community in Rio de Janeiro. On 11 March 1831, in what became known as the [Night of the Bottle Fight](/wiki/Night_of_the_Bottle_Fight "Night of the Bottle Fight") ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Noite das Garrafadas*), the Portuguese retaliated and turmoil gripped the streets of the national capital.[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986246–247-197)[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988158-198) On 5 April, Pedro I fired the Liberal cabinet, which had only been in power since 19 March, for its incompetence in restoring order.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988159-192)[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986250-199) A large crowd, incited by the radicals, gathered in Rio de Janeiro downtown on the afternoon of 6 April and demanded the immediate restoration of the fallen cabinet.[[195]](#cite_note-200) The Emperor's reply was: "I will do everything for the people and nothing [compelled] by the people."[[196]](#cite_note-201) Sometime after nightfall, army troops, including his guard, deserted him and joined the protests. Only then did he realize how isolated and detached from Brazilian affairs he had become, and to everyone's surprise, he abdicated at approximately 03:00 on 7 April.[[197]](#cite_note-202) Upon delivering the abdication document to a messenger, he said: "Here you have my act of abdication, I'm returning to Europe and leaving a country that I loved very much, and still love."[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986252-203)[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_3114-204)

## Return to Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_I_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Return to Europe")]

### Portuguese Civil War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_I_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Portuguese Civil War")]

Main article: [Liberal Wars](/wiki/Liberal_Wars "Liberal Wars")

[![Lithographic half-length portrait depicting a middle-aged man with a full beard and wearing a military tunic with epaulets, sash and large medal](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Pedro_I_of_Brazil_1833.jpg/250px-Pedro_I_of_Brazil_1833.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pedro_I_of_Brazil_1833.jpg)

Pedro, Duke of Braganza, at age 35, 1833. After their invasion of Portugal, he and his soldiers swore not to shave their beards until [Maria II](/wiki/Maria_II_of_Portugal "Maria II of Portugal") was restored.[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELustosa2006323-205)

At dawn on the morning of 7 April, Pedro, his wife, and others, including his daughter Maria II and his sister Ana de Jesus, were taken on board the British warship [HMS *Warspite* (1807)](/wiki/HMS_Warspite_(1807) "HMS Warspite (1807)"). The vessel remained at anchor off Rio de Janeiro, and, on 13 April, the former emperor transferred to and departed for Europe aboard [HMS *Volage* (1825)](/wiki/HMS_Volage_(1825) "HMS Volage (1825)").[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986254–257-206)[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_3117,_119,_142–143-207) He arrived in [Cherbourg-Octeville](/wiki/Cherbourg-Octeville "Cherbourg-Octeville"), France, on 10 June.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986257-208)[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_3149,_151-209) During the next few months, he shuttled between France and Great Britain. He was warmly welcomed, but received no actual support from either government to restore his daughter's throne.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986257–260,_262-210) Finding himself in an awkward situation because he held no official status in either the Brazilian Imperial House or in the Portuguese Royal House, Pedro assumed the title of [Duke of Braganza](/wiki/Duke_of_Braganza "Duke of Braganza") on 15 June, a position that once had been his as heir-apparent to Portugal's crown. Although the title should have belonged to Maria II's heir-apparent, which he certainly was not, his claim was met with general recognition.[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_3158-211)[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986259-212) On 1 December, his only daughter by Amélie, [Maria Amélia](/wiki/Princess_Maria_Am%C3%A9lia_of_Brazil "Princess Maria Amélia of Brazil"), was born in Paris.[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986267-213)

He did not forget his children left in Brazil. He wrote poignant letters to each of them, conveying how greatly he missed them and repeatedly asking them to seriously attend to their educations. Shortly before his abdication, Pedro had told his son and successor: "I intend that my brother Miguel and I will be the last badly educated of the Braganza family".[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988281-214)[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon197536-215) [Charles Napier](/wiki/Charles_Napier_(Royal_Navy_officer) "Charles Napier (Royal Navy officer)"), a naval commander who fought under Pedro's banner in the 1830s, remarked that "his good qualities were his own; his bad owing to want of education; and no man was more sensible of that defect than himself."[[211]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECosta1995117-216)[[212]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJorge1972203-217) His letters to Pedro II were often couched in language beyond the boy's reading level, and historians have assumed such passages were chiefly intended as advice that the young monarch might eventually consult upon reaching adulthood.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986257-208)[[f]](#cite_note-219)

While in Paris, the Duke of Braganza met and befriended [Gilbert du Motier, Marquis of Lafayette](/wiki/Gilbert_du_Motier,_marquis_de_Lafayette "Gilbert du Motier, marquis de Lafayette"), a veteran of the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") who became one of his staunchest supporters.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986259-212)[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELustosa2006306-220) With limited funds, Pedro organized a small army composed of Portuguese liberals, like [Almeida Garrett](/wiki/Almeida_Garrett "Almeida Garrett") and [Alexandre Herculano](/wiki/Alexandre_Herculano "Alexandre Herculano"), foreign mercenaries and volunteers such as Lafayette's grandson, Adrien Jules de Lasteyrie.[[215]](#cite_note-221) On 25 January 1832, Pedro bade farewell to his family, Lafayette and around two hundred well-wishers. He knelt before Maria II and said: "My lady, here is a Portuguese general who will uphold your rights and restore your crown." In tears, his daughter embraced him.[[216]](#cite_note-222) Pedro and his army sailed to the Atlantic archipelago of the [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores"), the only Portuguese territory that had remained loyal to his daughter. After a few months of final preparations they embarked for mainland Portugal, entering the city of [Porto](/wiki/Porto "Porto") unopposed on 9 July. His brother's troops moved to encircle the city, [beginning a siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Porto "Siege of Porto") that lasted for more than a year.[[217]](#cite_note-223)

### Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_I_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Death")]

See also: [First Carlist War](/wiki/First_Carlist_War "First Carlist War")

[![A lithograph depicting a curtained bed on which lies a bearded man with closed eyes and a crucifix lying on his chest](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Pedro_I_of_Brazil_dead_1834.jpg/250px-Pedro_I_of_Brazil_dead_1834.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pedro_I_of_Brazil_dead_1834.jpg)

Pedro on his deathbed, 1834

In early 1833, while besieged in Porto, Pedro received news from Brazil of his daughter [Paula](/wiki/Princess_Paula_of_Brazil "Princess Paula of Brazil")'s impending death.[[g]](#cite_note-225) Months later, in September, he met with [Antônio Carlos de Andrada](/wiki/Ant%C3%B4nio_Carlos_Ribeiro_de_Andrada "Antônio Carlos Ribeiro de Andrada"), a brother of Bonifácio who had come from Brazil. As a representative of the Restorationist Party, Antônio Carlos asked the Duke of Braganza to return to Brazil and rule his former empire as regent during his son's minority. Pedro realized that the Restorationists wanted to use him as a tool to facilitate their own rise to power, and frustrated Antônio Carlos by making several demands, to ascertain whether the Brazilian people, and not merely a faction, truly wanted him back. He insisted that any request to return as regent be constitutionally valid. The people's will would have to be conveyed through their local representatives and his appointment approved by the General Assembly. Only then, and "upon the presentation of a petition to him in Portugal by an official delegation of the Brazilian parliament" would he consider accepting.[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986293-226)[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_3287-227)

During the war, the Duke of Braganza mounted cannons, dug trenches, tended the wounded, ate among the rank and file and fought under heavy fire as men next to him were shot or blown to pieces.[[221]](#cite_note-228) His cause was nearly lost until he took the risky step of dividing his forces and sending a portion to launch an [amphibious attack](/wiki/Amphibious_attack "Amphibious attack") on southern Portugal. The [Algarve](/wiki/Algarve "Algarve") region fell to the expedition, which then marched north straight for Lisbon, which capitulated on 24 July.[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986290-229) Pedro proceeded to subdue the remainder of the country, but just when the conflict looked to be winding down to a conclusion, his Spanish uncle Don [Carlos](/wiki/Infante_Carlos,_Count_of_Molina "Infante Carlos, Count of Molina"), who was attempting to seize the crown of his niece Doña [Isabella II](/wiki/Isabella_II_of_Spain "Isabella II of Spain"), intervened. In this wider conflict that engulfed the entire [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula"), the [First Carlist War](/wiki/First_Carlist_War "First Carlist War"), the Duke of Braganza allied with liberal Spanish armies loyal to Isabella II and defeated both Miguel I and Carlos. A [peace accord was reached](/wiki/Concession_of_Evoramonte "Concession of Evoramonte") on 26 May 1834.[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986295,_297–298-230)[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_3291,_293–294-231)

Except for bouts of [epilepsy](/wiki/Epilepsy "Epilepsy") that manifested in [seizures](/wiki/Seizures "Seizures") every few years, Pedro had always enjoyed robust health.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay198636-35)[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELustosa200672–73-232) The war, however, undermined his constitution and by 1834 he was dying of [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis").[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986302-233) He was confined to his bed in Queluz Royal Palace from 10 September.[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986304-234)[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_3302-235) Pedro dictated an open letter to the Brazilians, in which he begged that a gradual abolition of slavery be adopted. He warned them: "Slavery is an evil, and an attack against the rights and dignity of the human species, but its consequences are less harmful to those who suffer in captivity than to the Nation whose laws allow slavery. It is a cancer that devours its morality."[[229]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJorge1972198–199-236) After a long and painful illness, Pedro died at 14:30 on 24 September 1834.[[230]](#cite_note-237) As he had requested, [his heart](/wiki/Dom_Pedro_I_heart "Dom Pedro I heart") was placed in Porto's Lapa Church and his body was interred in the [Royal Pantheon of the House of Braganza](/wiki/Royal_Pantheon_of_the_House_of_Braganza "Royal Pantheon of the House of Braganza").[[231]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986305-238)[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_3309-239) The news of his death arrived in Rio de Janeiro on 20 November, but his children were informed only after 2 December.[[233]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1999433-240) Bonifácio, who had been removed from his position as their guardian, wrote to Pedro II and his sisters: "Dom Pedro did not die. Only ordinary men die, not heroes."[[234]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986299-241)[[235]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon197581-242)

Upon the death of Pedro I, the then-powerful Restorationist Party vanished overnight.[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988178-243) A fair assessment of the former monarch became possible once the threat of his return to power was removed. [Evaristo da Veiga](/wiki/Evaristo_da_Veiga "Evaristo da Veiga"), one of his worst critics as well as a leader in the Liberal Party, left a statement which, according to historian Octávio Tarquínio de Sousa, became the prevailing view thereafter:[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_3309-239) "the former emperor of Brazil was not a prince of ordinary measure ... and Providence has made him a powerful instrument of liberation, both in Brazil and in Portugal. If we [Brazilians] exist as a body in a free Nation, if our land was not ripped apart into small enemy republics, where only anarchy and military spirit predominated, we owe much to the resolution he took in remaining among us, in making the first shout for our Independence." He continued: "Portugal, if it was freed from the darkest and demeaning tyranny ... if it enjoys the benefits brought by representative government to learned peoples, it owes it to D[om]. Pedro de Alcântara, whose fatigues, sufferings and sacrifices for the Portuguese cause has earned him in high degree the tribute of national gratitude."[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJorge1972204-244)[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_3309,_312-245)

John Armitage, who lived in Brazil during the latter half of [Pedro I's reign](/wiki/First_reign_(Empire_of_Brazil) "First reign (Empire of Brazil)"), remarked that "even the errors of the Monarch have been attended with great benefit through their influence on the affairs of the mother country. Had he governed with more wisdom it would have been well for the land of his adoption, yet, perhaps, unfortunate for humanity." Armitage added that like "the [late Emperor of the French](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon"), he was also a child of destiny, or rather, an instrument in the hands of an all-seeing and beneficent Providence for the furtherance of great and inscrutable ends. In the old as in the new world he was henceforth fated to become the instrument of further revolutions, and ere the close of his brilliant but ephemeral career in the land of his fathers, to atone amply for the errors and follies of his former life, by his chivalrous and heroic devotion in the cause of civil and religious freedom."[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArmitage_1836,_Vol_2139–140-246)

In 1972, on the 150th anniversary of Brazilian independence, Pedro I's remains (though not his heart) were brought to Brazil—as he had requested in his will—accompanied by much fanfare and with honors due to a head of state. His remains were reinterred in the [Monument to the Independence of Brazil](/wiki/Monument_to_the_Independence_of_Brazil "Monument to the Independence of Brazil"), along with those of Maria Leopoldina and Amélie, in the city of São Paulo.[[231]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986305-238)[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon1975900-247) Years later, [Neill Macaulay](/wiki/Neill_W._Macaulay_Jr. "Neill W. Macaulay Jr.") said that "[c]riticism of Dom Pedro was freely expressed and often vehement; it prompted him to abdicate two thrones. His tolerance of public criticism and his willingness to relinquish power set Dom Pedro apart from his absolutist predecessors and from the rulers of today's coercive states, whose lifetime tenure is as secure as that of the kings of old." Macaulay affirmed that "[s]uccessful liberal leaders like Dom Pedro may be honored with an occasional stone or bronze monument, but their portraits, four stories high, do not shape public buildings; their pictures are not borne in parades of hundreds of thousands of uniformed marchers; no '-isms' attach to their names."[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986x-248)

As part of Brazil's 200-year independence celebrations in 2022, the embalmed heart of Pedro I received military honors upon arrival in [Brasília](/wiki/Bras%C3%ADlia "Brasília") and was put on public display at the [foreign ministry](/wiki/Itamaraty_Palace "Itamaraty Palace"). It was then returned to Portugal after [Brazil's independence day](/wiki/Independence_Day_(Brazil) "Independence Day (Brazil)").[[242]](#cite_note-249)[[243]](#cite_note-250)
