## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_I_of_Brazil&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** Full name Pedro de Alcântara Francisco António João Carlos Xavier de Paula Miguel Rafael Joaquim José Gonzaga Pascoal Cipriano Serafim.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBranco1838XXXVI-1)[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon19753-2)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** Pedro I became known as "the Liberator" in Brazil for his role in the country's independence.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEViana1994252-4) He also became known as "the Liberator" in Portugal, as well as "the Soldier King". Both epithets resulted from his part in [the war against his brother](/wiki/Liberal_Wars "Liberal Wars"), Dom [Miguel I](/wiki/Miguel_I_of_Portugal "Miguel I of Portugal").[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaraiva2001378-5)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-93)** Of the two eyewitness accounts of 7 September 1822 that mention Prince Dom Pedro's mount, that of Father Belquior Pinheiro de Oliveira on 7 September 1826 and that of Manuel Marcondes de Oliveira e Melo (later Baron of Pindamonhangaba) on 14 April 1862, both say that it was a *besta baia* (bay beast).[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECosta_1972,_Vol_29574,_80-83) In a work published in 1853 and based on an interview with another eyewitness, Colonel Antônio Leite Pereira da Gama Lobo, historian Paulo Antônio do Vale said that it was a *zaino* ([bay horse](/wiki/Bay_horse "Bay horse")).[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECosta_1972,_Vol_29575,_80-84) The words used, "bay beast" and "bay horse", are at first glance, both similar and confusingly vague. In Portuguese, as in English, a beast is any non-human animal especially a large four-footed mammal. However, formerly in Brazil beast also meant "[mare](/wiki/Mare "Mare")" (a female horse) as may be seen in dictionaries published in 1946[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFreira19461022-85) and 1968,[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarvalho1968158-86) although this usage has since been discontinued except in Brazil's [northeast](/wiki/Northeast_Region,_Brazil "Northeast Region, Brazil") and [northern](/wiki/North_Region,_Brazil "North Region, Brazil") regions.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHouaissVillar2009281-87) The use of *besta* to refer to a mare is still in use in Portugal.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDicionários_Editora1997205-88) Thus, the description of a "bay horse" with non-defined sex and "bay beast (mare)" do in fact coincide. Two of Pedro's biographers, [Pedro Calmon](/w/index.php?title=Pedro_Calmon&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pedro Calmon (page does not exist)")[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon197597-89) and [Neill Macaulay](/wiki/Neill_W._Macaulay,_Jr. "Neill W. Macaulay, Jr.")[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1986125-90) have identified his horse as a bay mare. Pedro, who was an outstanding horseman and making an average of 108 kilometres (67 mi) per day, was able to ride this animal from the city of São Paulo back to the capital of Rio de Janeiro in just five days, leaving his guard and his entourage far behind.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECosta_1972,_Vol_295131-91) Francisco Gomes da Silva, "the Buffoon", the second person to arrive, lagged behind the Prince by some eight hours.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECosta_1972,_Vol_295133-92)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-125)** Pedro I gave up more than just the crowns of Portugal and Brazil. Less known is that he was also offered the crown of Greece in April 1822 (while he was still prince regent) by the Greek government which was embroiled in a [fight for national independence](/wiki/Greek_War_of_Independence "Greek War of Independence"). Pedro I declined, and eventually [Otto of Bavaria](/wiki/Otto_of_Greece "Otto of Greece") became [King of Greece](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Greece_(Wittelsbach) "Kingdom of Greece (Wittelsbach)").[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECosta1995172–173-122) Pedro I also declined offers of the Spanish crown made in 1826 and 1829 by liberals who rebelled against the absolutist rule of his uncle, Don [Ferdinand VII](/wiki/Ferdinand_VII "Ferdinand VII"). The liberals in Portugal and Spain agreed in 1830 to make Pedro I the "Emperor of Iberia". He seems to have declined this offer also, since nothing came of it.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECosta1995195–197-123) Brazilian historian Sérgio Corrêa da Costa and Portuguese historian Antônio Sardinha have argued, however, with little supporting evidence, that one of the inducements which prompted Pedro I to abdicate the Brazilian crown was to dethrone his brother and his uncle and rule the entire [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula") as its emperor.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECosta1995197,_199-124)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-152)** Rumors circulated at the time purporting that Pedro I had kicked Maria Leopoldina in the womb during a heated discussion. The quarrel was witnessed by Domitila de Castro and Wenzel Philipp Leopold, Baron von Mareschal. Then serving Maria Leopoldina's father as the Austrian minister in Brazil and thus inclined to reflect her interests, Mareschal was the sole eyewitness who left an account of what actually happened. According to him, the couple had a bitter argument in which they exchanged insults, but there is no mention of physical violence.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERangel1928162–163Calmon197514–15Costa199586-145) Historians Alberto Rangel[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERangel1928163-146), Pedro Calmon,[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalmon1950137Calmon197514-147) Octávio Tarquínio de Sousa,[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_2242-148), Sérgio Corrêa da Costa[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECosta199586-149) and Roderick J. Barman[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman199917-32) have rejected the possibility that Pedro I physically harmed his wife and all affirmed that the altercation was limited to harsh language. A later exhumation confirmed that Maria Leopoldina had died of natural causes.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETavares2013-150) As late as 1831, however, aspersions on Pedro's conduct at the time of his wife's death were still being whispered, serving as a lasting reminder of what people actually believed, regardless of the unfounded nature of the allegations.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESousa_1972,_Vol_2242-148) Barman was categorical when he noted that Maria Leopoldina's death stripped Pedro I of "any remaining aura of sanctity, either at home or abroad".[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarman1988147-151)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-219)** A notable passage in a missive to Pedro II gives an insight into the Duke of Braganza's political philosophy: "The era in which princes were respected solely because they are simply princes has ended; in the century in which we live, in which the peoples are quite well informed of their rights, it is necessary that princes should be and also should know that they are men and not divinities, that for them knowledge and good sense are indispensable so that they are the more quickly loved than respected. The respect of a free people for their ruler ought to be born of the conviction which they hold that their ruler is capable of making them achieve that level of happiness they aspire to; and if such is not the case, unhappy ruler, unhappy people"[[213]](#cite_note-218)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-225)** Pedro made two requests to José Bonifácio, his children's guardian: "the first is to keep for me a bit of her beautiful hair; the second is to place her in the convent of *Nossa Senhora da Ajuda* ["Our Lady of Good Aid"] and in the same spot where her good mother, my Leopoldina for whom even today I still shed tears of longing, is located ... I ask you as a father, as a pitiful desolate father, to do me a favor and go in person to deposit next to the body of her mother this fruit of her womb and on this occasion pray for one and other".[[218]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESantos201129-224)
